events.text: The patient is a 58-year-old male named Raymond Kowalski. He is a former smoker (20 pack-year history, quit 8 years prior), works as a high school history teacher, and has a past medical history of hypertension (well-controlled on lisinopril 10 mg daily), hyperlipidemia (atorvastatin 20 mg daily), and type 2 diabetes mellitus (metformin 1000 mg twice daily). He is of Polish-American descent. BMI is 27.4 kg/m2. He has no family history of colorectal cancer. ECOG performance status 1.

At age 58 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition, cT4aN2bM1a) ADENOCARCINOMA OF THE SIGMOID COLON (ICD-O-3 site code C18.7, histology code 8140/3). The tumor was identified as a left-sided, moderately differentiated (grade 2) adenocarcinoma. Staging workup revealed synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases (4 lesions; largest 3.8 cm in segment VI, additional lesions in segments II, IV, and VII). No peritoneal disease or extrahepatic nodal metastases were identified at initial staging. Summary stage: Distant. Relevant biomarkers: KRAS exon 2, codon 12, p.G12D mutation (positive); NRAS wild-type; BRAF V600E wild-type; HER2 non-amplified; MMR-proficient (MLH1+, MSH2+, MSH6+, PMS2+ by IHC), MSS by PCR; tumor mutational burden (TMB) 6.2 mut/Mb (low). CEA at diagnosis: 187.4 ng/mL. CA 19-9 at diagnosis: 412 U/mL. Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY (colonoscopy biopsy of sigmoid mass and CT-guided biopsy of hepatic lesion). Tumor size: 4.5 cm (primary sigmoid mass). Lymph nodes examined: 0 (clinical staging). The primary tumor caused partial colonic obstruction. RAS/RAF mutation status precludes use of anti-EGFR therapy.

At age 58 years, the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY from colonoscopic biopsy of sigmoid mass. Gross description: multiple mucosal biopsies, tan-pink, aggregate 1.2 cm. Microscopic findings: moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with glandular architecture, nuclear pleomorphism, and frequent mitoses. Tumor cells show intraluminal dirty necrosis characteristic of colorectal origin. No signet ring cell features. Immunohistochemistry: CDX2 positive, CK20 positive, CK7 negative, consistent with colorectal primary. Mismatch repair proteins intact (MLH1+, MSH2+, MSH6+, PMS2+) indicating proficient MMR status. Separate CT-guided liver biopsy: metastatic adenocarcinoma, morphologically consistent with colorectal primary. Same IHC profile confirmed.

At age 58 years, the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (staging CT) which showed cancer. Primary sigmoid colon mass measuring 4.5 x 3.8 cm with mesorectal/mesenteric fat stranding and involvement of at least 4 pericolonic lymph nodes (largest 1.4 cm). Liver: 4 hypodense hepatic lesions in bilobar distribution: segment VI (3.8 x 3.1 cm), segment II (1.9 x 1.6 cm), segment IV (2.2 x 1.8 cm), segment VII (1.1 x 0.9 cm). No evidence of pulmonary nodules. No peritoneal implants. No adrenal or osseous metastases. Impression: Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases. The hepatic disease burden is potentially resectable after adequate tumor response to systemic therapy.

At age 58 years, the patient had a PET-CT (staging PET scan) which showed cancer at multiple sites. FDG-avid sigmoid colon primary mass (SUVmax 14.2). FDG-avid hepatic metastases in segments II, IV, VI, and VII (SUVmax range 8.7-12.4). No FDG-avid pulmonary nodules. No FDG-avid peritoneal implants. No FDG-avid osseous or adrenal lesions. Impression: findings consistent with CT staging; no occult metastatic disease identified beyond hepatic involvement.

At age 58 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for colorectal adenocarcinoma based on a specimen obtained from the sigmoid colon primary tumor (formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded tissue). Sequencing platform: comprehensive 500-gene panel with TMB and MSI analysis. Key findings: KRAS p.G12D mutation (variant allele frequency 42.3%, pathogenic, oncogenic driver); TP53 p.R248W mutation (VAF 39.1%, pathogenic); APC p.Q1367* (VAF 44.7%, pathogenic, truncating); SMAD4 p.R361H (VAF 18.2%, pathogenic, potential marker of poor prognosis and metastatic potential); PIK3CA p.E545K (VAF 11.4%, pathogenic, potentially actionable); FBXW7 p.R465C (VAF 9.8%, likely pathogenic); TCF7L2 p.E225* (VAF 8.1%, variant of uncertain significance); ARID1A p.Q2197* (VAF 7.3%, likely pathogenic); Copy number alterations: MYC amplification (8q24), CCND1 gain, CDKN2A homozygous deletion; Gene fusions: none detected. TMB: 6.2 mut/Mb (low). MSI: microsatellite stable (MSS). NRAS: wild-type (exons 2, 3, 4). BRAF: wild-type (exons 11, 15). HER2: not amplified (copy number 2.1). RET: wild-type. ALK: wild-type. NTRK1/2/3: wild-type. Actionable findings summary: KRAS G12D is an oncogenic driver precluding anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab); PIK3CA E545K may be relevant for future clinical trial enrollment; SMAD4 loss associated with limited benefit from anti-VEGF therapy in some analyses but does not preclude bevacizumab use; KRAS G12D inhibitors (e.g., sotorasib, adagrasib - colon data limited) noted for future consideration.

At age 58 years, the patient had an initial oncology consultation. Multidisciplinary tumor board review conducted. Discussed KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS, Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with bilobar hepatic metastases. Hepatic disease deemed potentially convertible to resectability following systemic therapy response. Anti-EGFR therapy not an option given RAS mutation. Plan: initiate FOLFOX (mFOLFOX6) plus bevacizumab with intent to achieve sufficient tumor response for possible hepatic resection consideration after 6-8 cycles. Baseline blood pressure noted as 138/88 mmHg (slightly elevated above his typical controlled baseline). Baseline peripheral sensation intact. Discussed risks of bevacizumab (hypertension, wound healing) and oxaliplatin (neurotoxicity, myelosuppression). Patient agreeable and motivated. CEA: 187.4 ng/mL. CA 19-9: 412 U/mL. Labs: CBC WNL, CMP WNL, eGFR 74 mL/min. ECOG PS 1.

At age 58 years, the patient received palliative-intent mFOLFOX6 (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2 IV day 1, leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1, 5-fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus day 1 then 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours) plus bevacizumab 5 mg/kg IV day 1, every 14 days. This was the initiation of first-line chemotherapy. The primary tumor was left in place (patient declined diverting colostomy given only partial obstruction and adequate bowel function on high-fiber diet). Bevacizumab was held for the first cycle due to planned liver biopsy healing considerations (10 days prior).

At age 58 years (after cycle 3 of mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab), the patient developed Grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy (mild paresthesias in fingertips bilaterally, no functional impairment). Also noted: Grade 2 fatigue, Grade 1 nausea (well-controlled with ondansetron and dexamethasone premedication). Blood pressure at cycle 3 visit: 152/96 mmHg (new Grade 1 treatment-related hypertension attributed to bevacizumab). Lisinopril dose increased to 20 mg daily. Cold sensitivity (cold-induced dysesthesias) characteristic of oxaliplatin noted, managed with cold avoidance counseling. No dose modifications made at this time.

At age 58 years (after 6 cycles of mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab, approximately 3 months into treatment), the patient had a CT ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (restaging) which showed cancer with response. Hepatic metastases: all four lesions demonstrate marked decrease in size - segment VI lesion now 2.1 x 1.7 cm (previously 3.8 x 3.1 cm, 45% reduction), segment II lesion 0.9 x 0.7 cm (previously 1.9 x 1.6 cm, 53% reduction), segment IV lesion 1.1 x 0.9 cm (previously 2.2 x 1.8 cm, 50% reduction), segment VII lesion 0.5 x 0.4 cm (previously 1.1 x 0.9 cm, 55% reduction). Primary sigmoid mass: 3.2 x 2.6 cm (previously 4.5 x 3.8 cm, 31% reduction). No new lesions. No pulmonary nodules. Overall RECIST 1.1 assessment: Partial Response (sum of target lesions decreased 48%). CEA: 54.2 ng/mL (down from 187.4). CA 19-9: 138 U/mL (down from 412).

At age 58 years (mid-treatment restaging, after cycle 6), the patient had an oncologist office assessment, which showed cancer with response to therapy. Excellent partial response to mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab after 6 cycles. Discussed potential hepatic resection candidacy at multidisciplinary tumor board. Hepatic surgery team reviewed imaging: with continued response, hepatic resection may become feasible, but primary sigmoid tumor resection would need to be addressed simultaneously or staged. Patient reports escalating peripheral neuropathy. Current neuropathy: Grade 2 (moderate paresthesias, interfering with fine motor tasks such as grading papers, but no functional disability for ADLs). Discussed oxaliplatin cumulative neurotoxicity - threshold typically reached around cycles 8-10. Blood pressure 158/102 mmHg on increased lisinopril - amlodipine 5 mg daily added. Plan: continue mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab for cycles 7-12 (total 12 cycles) with close neuropathy monitoring. Consider oxaliplatin dose reduction to 65 mg/m2 if neuropathy worsens to Grade 3. ECOG PS 1.

At age 58 years (after cycle 9 of mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab), the patient developed Grade 3 oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy (severe paresthesias and numbness in hands and feet; unable to button shirts or use keyboard without difficulty; mild gait instability on uneven surfaces). Oxaliplatin dose was reduced to 65 mg/m2 (from 85 mg/m2) starting with cycle 10 per FOLFOX neuropathy management guidelines. 5-FU and bevacizumab doses maintained. Neurology consultation obtained; EMG/nerve conduction studies showed moderate length-dependent axonal sensorimotor polyneuropathy consistent with platinum-induced peripheral neuropathy. Prescribed duloxetine 30 mg daily (uptitrated to 60 mg daily after 2 weeks) for neuropathic pain management. Blood pressure now 162/104 mmHg despite dual antihypertensive therapy - amlodipine uptitrated to 10 mg daily. Persistent Grade 2 hypertension attributed to bevacizumab.

At age 58 years (after 12 cycles of mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab, approximately 6 months into treatment), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (end-of-induction restaging) which showed cancer with partial response maintained. Hepatic metastases: segment VI lesion now 1.6 x 1.3 cm (previously 2.1 x 1.7 cm after 6 cycles), segment II lesion 0.6 x 0.5 cm (stable from cycle 6 restaging), segment IV lesion 0.8 x 0.6 cm (stable), segment VII lesion no longer measureable (near-complete response). Primary sigmoid mass: 2.4 x 1.9 cm (previously 3.2 x 2.6 cm). No pulmonary nodules. No peritoneal disease. RECIST 1.1 assessment: Partial Response maintained (cumulative 58% reduction in sum of target lesions from baseline). CT chest: no pulmonary nodules or lesions. CEA: 28.6 ng/mL. CA 19-9: 72 U/mL. Multidisciplinary tumor board re-reviewed: hepatic lesions technically resectable, but surgeon notes proximity of segment II lesion to left hepatic vein; resection feasible but with significant margin concerns. Patient wishes to continue systemic therapy given ongoing response.

At age 58 years, the patient had an oncologist office assessment after completing 12 cycles of mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab. Cancer present with partial response maintained. Reviewed multidisciplinary tumor board recommendation: patient and hepatic surgery team mutually agree to continue systemic therapy rather than proceed to hepatic resection at this time (ongoing response, patient preference, margin concerns for segment II lesion). Given Grade 3 neuropathy that has partially improved to Grade 2 after oxaliplatin dose reduction, decision made to transition to maintenance therapy: 5-FU/leucovorin (simplified LV5FU2 regimen) plus bevacizumab 5 mg/kg every 14 days (oxaliplatin stopped, consistent with OPTIMOX1 strategy). Neuropathy expected to further improve off oxaliplatin. Current blood pressure: 148/92 mmHg (improved slightly). ECOG PS 1. Duloxetine continued. Plan: maintenance LV5FU2 plus bevacizumab every 2 weeks; restage with CT every 8-10 weeks. CEA: 28.6 ng/mL.

At age 58 years (approximately 6 months after initial diagnosis), the patient received maintenance LV5FU2 (leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1, 5-fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus day 1 then 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours) plus bevacizumab 5 mg/kg IV every 14 days. This represented a planned transition from induction mFOLFOX6 to maintenance therapy following 12 cycles of oxaliplatin-based therapy. Oxaliplatin discontinued due to cumulative peripheral neuropathy (Grade 2-3).

At age 58 years (approximately 8 months after diagnosis, during maintenance LV5FU2 plus bevacizumab), the patient had a CT ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (maintenance restaging) which showed cancer with stable disease. Hepatic metastases: segment VI lesion 1.5 x 1.2 cm (stable), segment II lesion 0.6 x 0.5 cm (stable), segment IV lesion 0.7 x 0.5 cm (stable), segment VII lesion not visualized (complete response of this lesion). Primary sigmoid mass: 2.3 x 1.8 cm (stable). No new hepatic lesions. No pulmonary nodules. CEA: 31.2 ng/mL (slight increase but within assay variability). CA 19-9: 78 U/mL (slight increase). Overall assessment: stable disease on maintenance therapy.

At age 58-59 years (approximately 10 months after initial diagnosis, during maintenance LV5FU2 plus bevacizumab), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (restaging, prompted by rising tumor markers) which showed cancer with progression. New findings: 3 new bilateral pulmonary nodules - right upper lobe 8 mm, right lower lobe 6 mm, left upper lobe 7 mm - all highly suspicious for metastatic disease given clinical context. Hepatic disease: segment VI lesion now 2.4 x 2.1 cm (increased from 1.5 x 1.2 cm, 60% increase), segment II lesion 1.2 x 1.0 cm (increased from 0.6 x 0.5 cm, 100% increase), segment IV lesion 1.4 x 1.1 cm (increased from 0.7 x 0.5 cm, 120% increase). Primary sigmoid mass: 3.1 x 2.5 cm (increased from 2.3 x 1.8 cm, 39% increase). New small perihepatic lymphadenopathy (largest 1.3 cm). RECIST 1.1 assessment: Progressive Disease (new pulmonary lesions plus >20% increase in sum of target hepatic lesions). CEA: 94.7 ng/mL (rising from 31.2 one month prior). CA 19-9: 287 U/mL.

At age 58-59 years (10 months after initial diagnosis), the patient had an oncologist office assessment, which showed cancer with progression. Radiographic progression on maintenance LV5FU2 plus bevacizumab with new pulmonary metastases and hepatic lesion growth. CEA has risen markedly to 94.7 ng/mL. Patient is symptomatic with new mild exertional dyspnea and increased fatigue (ECOG PS 1-2). Peripheral neuropathy has improved significantly off oxaliplatin (now Grade 1, mild fingertip numbness only). Blood pressure 155/98 mmHg. Discussed second-line therapy options: FOLFIRI (irinotecan-based) plus a vascular-targeted agent. Given prior bevacizumab exposure and data from RAISE trial, plan for FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab (anti-VEGFR2 antibody). Anti-EGFR therapy not an option (KRAS G12D mutant). TAS-102 (trifluridine/tipiracil) and regorafenib held for third-line. KRAS G12D targeted therapy (investigational) discussed - patient interested in clinical trials if available. No KRAS G12D-specific approved therapy available for CRC at this time. Plan: initiate FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab. Lung biopsy to confirm metastatic CRC (to rule out primary lung malignancy given smoking history). Nephrology input re: blood pressure management. ECOG PS 1.

At age 58-59 years, the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY from CT-guided biopsy of right upper lobe pulmonary nodule. Gross description: core biopsy, 1.4 cm aggregate. Microscopic findings: metastatic moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. Morphology consistent with colorectal primary (columnar glands with luminal necrosis, nuclear pseudostratification). Immunohistochemistry: CDX2 diffusely positive, CK20 positive, CK7 focally positive, TTF-1 negative, Napsin A negative. Findings consistent with metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma; primary lung adenocarcinoma excluded. KRAS G12D mutation confirmed on repeat liquid biopsy (ctDNA) at time of progression - VAF 3.8%.

At age 58-59 years (approximately 10.5 months after initial diagnosis), the patient received second-line FOLFIRI (irinotecan 180 mg/m2 IV day 1, leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1, 5-fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus day 1 then 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours) plus ramucirumab 8 mg/kg IV day 1, every 14 days. UGT1A1 genotyping performed prior to initiation: UGT1A1*1/*28 heterozygous (intermediate metabolizer); irinotecan started at 150 mg/m2 with plan to uptitrate if tolerated. This represented second-line therapy for progression on 5-FU/bevacizumab maintenance.

At age 59 years (after cycle 2 of FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab), the patient developed Grade 2 diarrhea (4-6 loose stools per day above baseline) and Grade 2 fatigue. Diarrhea managed with loperamide 4 mg initially then 2 mg after each loose stool (up to 16 mg/day per protocol). Irinotecan dose reduced to 135 mg/m2 (further reduction from 150 mg/m2 due to UGT1A1*28 heterozygosity and Grade 2 toxicity). Blood pressure worsened: 168/108 mmHg despite triple antihypertensive therapy (lisinopril 20 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, now adding hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily). Grade 3 hypertension attributed to ramucirumab (VEGFR2 inhibition). Oncology and cardiology co-management. No Grade 3-4 neutropenia. Grade 1 alopecia noted.

At age 59 years (after 4 cycles of FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab, approximately 13 months after initial diagnosis), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (restaging) which showed cancer with mixed response. Pulmonary nodules: right upper lobe nodule 7 mm (previously 8 mm, slight decrease), right lower lobe nodule 5 mm (previously 6 mm, slight decrease), left upper lobe nodule 6 mm (previously 7 mm, slight decrease). Hepatic lesions: segment VI lesion 2.1 x 1.8 cm (decreased from 2.4 x 2.1 cm, 13% reduction), segment II lesion 1.0 x 0.8 cm (decreased from 1.2 x 1.0 cm), segment IV lesion 1.2 x 0.9 cm (decreased from 1.4 x 1.1 cm). Primary sigmoid mass: 2.8 x 2.2 cm (slight decrease from 3.1 x 2.5 cm). No new lesions. RECIST 1.1: Partial Response (borderline, 24% reduction in sum of target lesions from progression nadir). CEA: 61.3 ng/mL (down from 94.7 ng/mL). CA 19-9: 198 U/mL (down from 287 U/mL). Comment: early signals of response to FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab.

At age 59 years (approximately 13 months after initial diagnosis), the patient had an oncologist office assessment, which showed cancer with response to second-line therapy. Early partial response to FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab after 4 cycles. Patient tolerating therapy reasonably well with managed toxicities. Persistent Grade 3 hypertension requiring triple antihypertensive therapy - blood pressure now 146/94 mmHg on lisinopril, amlodipine, and hydrochlorothiazide. Nephrology co-managing. Peripheral neuropathy now Grade 1 (mild distal fingertip numbness, not limiting function). Diarrhea managed with dietary modifications and as-needed loperamide. No treatment discontinuation needed. UGT1A1 heterozygosity continues to require careful irinotecan dose monitoring. Discussed ongoing goals of care - patient desires continued active treatment. ECOG PS 1. CEA trending down. Continuing FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab. Plan: restage every 8-10 weeks. Referral to KRAS G12D clinical trial registry maintained. CA 19-9: 198 U/mL.

At age 59 years (after cycle 6 of FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab, approximately 14-15 months after initial diagnosis), the patient was admitted for Grade 3 irinotecan-induced diarrhea (>7 stools/day above baseline, mild dehydration) and Grade 3 neutropenia (ANC 0.6 x 10^9/L). Patient was afebrile; no bacteremia on blood cultures. Managed with IV fluid resuscitation, loperamide, and empiric ciprofloxacin for febrile neutropenia prophylaxis (afebrile but ANC critically low). G-CSF (filgrastim) administered subcutaneously. Recovery over 5 days. Irinotecan dose permanently reduced to 120 mg/m2 for subsequent cycles. 5-FU dose unchanged. Ramucirumab continued. Oncology team considered switching to an irinotecan-free regimen but patient wished to continue given partial response.

At age 59 years (after 8 cycles of FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab, approximately 16 months after initial diagnosis), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (restaging) which showed cancer with stable disease. Pulmonary nodules: right upper lobe nodule 7 mm (stable), right lower lobe nodule 5 mm (stable), left upper lobe nodule 5 mm (slight decrease, now 5 mm). Hepatic lesions: segment VI lesion 2.0 x 1.7 cm (stable), segment II lesion 0.9 x 0.8 cm (stable), segment IV lesion 1.1 x 0.9 cm (stable). Primary sigmoid mass: 2.7 x 2.1 cm (stable). No new lesions. RECIST 1.1: Stable Disease (no progression from prior restaging nadir). CEA: 58.8 ng/mL (stable from 61.3 ng/mL). CA 19-9: 189 U/mL (stable). Impression: disease control on FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab after irinotecan dose reduction. Monitoring for progression.

At age 59 years (approximately 16 months after initial diagnosis), the patient had an oncologist office assessment, which showed cancer, neither responding nor progressing (stable disease on second-line therapy). Stable disease maintained on reduced-dose FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab. Toxicity profile manageable: Grade 1-2 diarrhea intermittently, Grade 1 fatigue, Grade 1 neuropathy (residual from oxaliplatin). Blood pressure 144/90 mmHg on triple therapy - improved, now Grade 2 hypertension (not Grade 3). ECOG PS 1. Discussed ongoing KRAS G12D targeted therapy landscape: adagrasib and sotorasib have limited colorectal data with lower response rates than lung cancer; KRYSTAL-10 trial and other KRAS G12D-specific trials under accrual. Patient enrolled in a KRAS G12D inhibitor plus binimetinib combination trial at a nearby NCI-designated cancer center (met eligibility criteria: KRAS G12D mutant CRC, ECOG 0-1, prior 5-FU and oxaliplatin, no prior KRAS-targeted therapy). Plan: transition to clinical trial protocol after current cycle completion, with continued monitoring. CEA: 58.8 ng/mL. Goals of care discussion: patient understands incurable diagnosis, desires aggressive treatment, completed advance directive.

At age 59 years (approximately 17 months after initial diagnosis), the patient underwent laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with primary anastomosis for local control of the primary sigmoid colon tumor. Indications: increasing local symptoms (intermittent partial obstruction, hematochezia), and pre-trial surgical staging. Operative findings: 2.7 cm sigmoid mass with pericolic fat involvement; no gross peritoneal disease; 3 mesenteric lymph nodes identified. Procedure: laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with primary anastomosis. Estimated blood loss 120 mL. No intraoperative complications. Bevacizumab held for 4 weeks pre-operatively and ramucirumab held per protocol given healing concerns.

At age 59 years, the patient had a pathology result of type SURGICAL PATHOLOGY from laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy specimen. Final diagnosis: Moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of sigmoid colon, ypT3N1b (treated, post-chemotherapy). Histologic tumor response: Grade 2 (partial response, >10% but <50% viable tumor cells remaining, consistent with moderate response to chemotherapy). Proximal and distal resection margins: negative. Circumferential radial margin: 2.1 mm (negative). Lymph nodes: 2/13 positive for metastatic adenocarcinoma (0.4 cm and 0.6 cm deposits). Lymphovascular invasion: present. Perineural invasion: present. No tumor perforation. Consistent with KRAS G12D-mutant colorectal adenocarcinoma with prior chemotherapy effect. Pathologic staging: ypT3N1bM1b (Stage IVB by AJCC 8th edition).

At age 59 years (post-operative follow-up, approximately 18 months after initial diagnosis), the patient had an oncologist office assessment, which showed cancer. Recovered well from laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy. Pathology confirmed ypT3N1b with partial histologic response. Two positive mesenteric lymph nodes noted. Metastatic disease in liver and lungs ongoing. Blood pressure 142/92 mmHg post-recovery (bevacizumab/ramucirumab held peri-operatively). ECOG PS 1. Peripheral neuropathy Grade 1 (stable residual). Patient has been enrolled in KRAS G12D inhibitor clinical trial and will start trial protocol following surgical recovery (4-6 weeks post-op). CEA 48.2 ng/mL (slightly lower, possibly reflecting primary tumor removal contribution). CA 19-9: 162 U/mL. Overall disease course: metastatic CRC, KRAS G12D mutant, MSS, now with liver and lung metastases, s/p sigmoid colectomy for local control, receiving investigational KRAS-targeted therapy via clinical trial. Prognosis discussed honestly - median OS for KRAS-mutant Stage IV CRC approximately 18-24 months from diagnosis; patient currently at 18 months and doing well functionally. Continued aggressive treatment approach consistent with patient's wishes.

documents.text:
HEMATOLOGY/MEDICAL ONCOLOGY
OFFICE VISIT - MID-TREATMENT RESTAGING NOTE

ATTENDING ONCOLOGIST: [Attending]
FELLOW/RESIDENT: [Fellow]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 58-year-old male with KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS, Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with bilobar hepatic metastases presenting for mid-treatment restaging review and cycle 7 planning following completion of 6 cycles of mFOLFOX6 plus bev.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Mr. Kowalski is a 58-year-old former smoker (20 pack-year history, quit approximately 8 years ago) with a history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus who was diagnosed with Stage IV (cT4aN2bM1a) moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases approximately 3 months ago. Molecular profiling of the primary tumor revealed KRAS p.G12D mutation (VAF 42.3%), co-occurring TP53 p.R248W, APC p.Q1367* truncating mutation, SMAD4 p.R361H, and PIK3CA p.E545K. Tumor was MSS by PCR with low TMB (6.2 mut/Mb). RAS mutation status precluded anti-EGFR therapy. He was initiated on mFOLFOX6 (Eloxatin 85 mg/m2 IV day 1, leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1, Adrucil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus day 1 then 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours) plus Avastin 5 mg/kg IV every 14 days with the dual intent of achieving disease control and potentially converting his hepatic metastases to a resectable state.

At today's visit he presents for review of restaging CT imaging obtained after cycle 6, and for discussion of the treatment plan going forward. He completed cycle 6 approximately 10 days ago.

On direct questioning, Mr. Kowalski reports an overall sense that his symptoms have improved compared to his pre-treatment baseline - he notes less abdominal discomfort and his bowel habits have been more regular. However, he raises significant concern about his hands. He describes worsening numbness and a "pins and needles" sensation in both hands that has now begun to interfere meaningfully with his work as a high school history teacher - specifically, he has difficulty grading papers by hand, picking up small objects from his desk, and manipulating buttons on his shirt in the morning. He denies frank weakness but acknowledges that fine motor tasks require more effort and concentration than before. He also notes mild bilateral foot numbness that does not currently affect his balance. Cold sensitivity remains bothersome; he avoids cold beverages and has been wearing gloves when opening the refrigerator. He denies falls or significant gait disturbance on flat surfaces, though he is more cautious on uneven ground.

He reports Grade 2 fatigue overall - he can still complete his workday but finds he needs to rest after school. Nausea is well-managed with his premedication regimen. He has not experienced significant vomiting. Appetite is somewhat reduced but he has been maintaining his weight; family has been bringing in home-cooked meals. He denies any symptoms of bowel obstruction - no frank obstruction, hematochezia has been minimal, no abdominal distension. He reports mild elevation in blood pressure readings at home (ranging 150-160/95-105 mmHg on his home cuff), which he understands is related to the Avastin.

Tumor markers were drawn as part of today's assessment: CEA 54.2 ng/mL (down from 187.4 ng/mL at diagnosis and from 81.3 ng/mL at cycle 3). CA 19-9 is 138 U/mL (down from 412 U/mL at diagnosis). Both are trending in the right direction and correlate well with the imaging findings.

Restaging CT abdomen/pelvis performed earlier this week is reviewed in detail below.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Fatigue Grade 2 (present, manageable), no fevers, no chills, no unintentional weight loss (weight stable at approximately 178 lbs)
- HEENT: No headache, no visual changes, no oral mucositis currently
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no dyspnea at rest; mild dyspnea on exertion with brisk walking (possible mild deconditioning vs. early anemia)
- Pulmonary: No cough, no hemoptysis
- GI: Mild nausea (well-controlled with premedication), no vomiting, no diarrhea currently, bowel movements 1-2 times/day (formed), no hematochezia, no melena, no abdominal pain; mild bloating occasionally
- GU: No hematuria, normal urinary stream
- Musculoskeletal: No bone pain
- Neurological: Bilateral peripheral paresthesias in hands and feet (Grade 2, described above); cold-induced dysesthesias; no cranial nerve symptoms
- Skin: No rash; minor hand-foot syndrome not present; mild hair thinning (not alopecia)
- Endocrine/Metabolic: Diabetes well-controlled per patient report
- Psychiatric: Mild situational anxiety, coping reasonably well; family support in place

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Stage IV (AJCC 8th ed., cT4aN2bM1a) moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of sigmoid colon with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases - KRAS G12D mutant, MSS, TMB-low - on active treatment
2. Hypertension - previously well-controlled on lisinopril 10 mg daily; now with treatment-related exacerbation (Avastin-associated), dose escalated to lisinopril 20 mg daily after cycle 3; most recent addition of amlodipine 5 mg daily this cycle
3. Hyperlipidemia - stable on atorvastatin 20 mg daily
4. Type 2 diabetes mellitus - managed with metformin 1000 mg twice daily; glucose control reported adequate per patient
5. Former smoker - 20 pack-years, quit 8 years prior

FAMILY HISTORY:
No known family history of colorectal cancer, inflammatory bowel disease, or hereditary cancer syndromes. Father had coronary artery disease. Mother had type 2 diabetes mellitus.

SOCIAL HISTORY:
High school history teacher; works full-time though functional capacity at work is mildly reduced. Married, lives with wife. Two adult children who are supportive. Former smoker, no current tobacco use. Occasional alcohol prior to diagnosis, now abstinent. No illicit drug use. Polish-American descent. Has reliable transportation to clinic. Wife accompanies to most visits.

ALLERGIES:
No known drug allergies. No prior adverse reactions to contrast.

MEDICATIONS (current):

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Mr. Kowalski is a 58-year-old male with KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS, Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases who has had an excellent partial response to mFOLFOX6 plus Avastin after 6 cycles. CEA and CA 19-9 have both declined substantially and correlate with the imaging findings. Multiple intersecting issues require attention today:

1. ONCOLOGIC STATUS - PARTIAL RESPONSE TO FIRST-LINE THERAPY
The imaging response is encouraging. All hepatic lesions have decreased substantially in size, with the segment VII lesion nearly gone. The primary sigmoid tumor has also responded. CEA has declined from 187.4 to 54.2 ng/mL and CA 19-9 from 412 to 138 U/mL - both reflecting continued treatment effect. I have discussed the CT results in detail with Mr. Kowalski and his wife. This was reviewed at today's multidisciplinary tumor board. The hepatic surgery colleagues have reviewed the imaging: with continued response, hepatic resection may become technically feasible. However, the hepatic surgery team noted ongoing concerns about the segment II lesion's proximity to the left hepatic vein; adequate margins would be challenging at the current stage, and they prefer to see additional response before committing to a resection plan. The primary sigmoid tumor would also need to be addressed - either staged (sigmoid colectomy first, then hepatic resection at a later date) or as a combined procedure, depending on patient status and surgeon preference. Given ongoing excellent response, the multidisciplinary team recommends continuing the current regimen for cycles 7 through 12 (with the goal of completing 12 total cycles of oxali-based induction), then reassessing resectability.

Plan: Continue mFOLFOX6 plus Avastin. Full restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis at 12-cycle mark. CEA and CA 19-9 with each cycle.

2. oxali-INDUCED PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY - NOW GRADE 2
This is an important management issue today. After 6 cycles of Eloxatin at 85 mg/m2, Mr. Kowalski has developed clear Grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy. He has moderate paresthesias and numbness in both hands, with meaningful interference in fine motor tasks (buttoning shirts, grading papers, keyboard use). He also has mild foot numbness. Exam confirms reduced distal sensation in fingertips, diminished vibratory sense in toes, and slightly wide-based gait - consistent with an early-to-moderate length-dependent sensorimotor picture, though predominantly sensory at this stage.

With cumulative oxali dosing, neuropathy typically worsens and the threshold for Grade 3 is often reached around cycles 8-12. The current Grade 2 presentation does not mandate dose reduction per standard guidelines, but the trajectory is concerning given the patient's occupation requires fine motor skills. I have had a detailed conversation with Mr. Kowalski about the trade-off between continuing at full dose (for maximum anti-tumor effect) vs. dose reduction (to preserve neurologic function). At this time, we will continue Eloxatin at 85 mg/m2 for cycle 7 but with a low threshold to reduce to 65 mg/m2 if symptoms worsen - specifically, if he develops difficulty with ADLs, gait instability affecting safety, or advancement to Grade 3 neuropathy. I have asked him to call the oncology nurse line immediately if his symptoms significantly worsen between cycles. Cold avoidance counseling reinforced.

Vitamin B6 use noted - patient counseled that evidence for neuropathy prevention is limited but no harm in continuing. Will review at next visit whether duloxetine is warranted; for now, neuropathy is not painful enough to justify initiation, but I have discussed this as an option if it becomes more bothersome.

Plan: Eloxatin maintained at 85 mg/m2 for cycle 7. Reduce to 65 mg/m2 if Grade 2 worsens or Grade 3 develops. Close monitoring at each visit. Consider early neurology referral if progression.

3. bev-ASSOCIATED HYPERTENSION
Blood pressure today is 158/102 mmHg. This is Grade 2 treatment-related hypertension attributed to Avastin (VEGF pathway inhibition). His baseline blood pressure was well-controlled on lisinopril 10 mg prior to treatment. Lisinopril was increased to 20 mg after cycle 3. Amlodipine 5 mg was added at that visit. Despite dual antihypertensive therapy, today's reading remains above goal. I am uptitrating amlodipine to 10 mg daily. I have explained to the patient that bev-related hypertension is a known class effect and that good blood pressure control is important to reduce cardiovascular risk, protect renal function (particularly relevant given his mild creatinine elevation and diabetes), and avoid forced discontinuation of bev. He will monitor BP at home daily and bring a log to next visit. If BP exceeds 160/100 consistently, we will need to add a third antihypertensive agent or consider cardiology/nephrology consultation. Avastin continues at 5 mg/kg as long as hypertension remains Grade 2 or below and is manageable.

Plan: Uptitrate amlodipine from 5 mg to 10 mg daily. Daily home BP monitoring. Threshold for additional intervention if BP persistently >160/100. Recheck creatinine and urinalysis next visit (proteinuria borderline at trace/1+ today but protein/creatinine ratio acceptable).

4. MILD ANEMIA
Hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL (down from 12.1 g/dL at cycle 3). This is a mild decline, likely multifactorial: myelosuppression from 5-FU/Eloxatin, possible chronic disease contribution from malignancy, and suboptimal erythropoietic drive. Patient is not symptomatic from anemia at rest; mild exertional dyspnea noted on ROS but this may also reflect deconditioning. Transfusion not required at this level. Will monitor. If hemoglobin falls below 9 g/dL or becomes symptomatic, will consider transfusion. ESA therapy generally avoided in curative or potentially resectable settings per ASCO guidelines; given this patient's trajectory toward a possible surgical intent, I will not initiate an ESA at this time.

Plan: Monitor CBC with each cycle. Dietary iron and folate counseling reinforced. Transfuse if symptomatic or Hgb < 9 g/dL.

5. MILD eGFR DECLINE
eGFR 69 mL/min (from 74 mL/min at baseline). This is a mild, possibly clinically insignificant change, but in the context of bev use, diabetic nephropathy risk, and borderline proteinuria, it warrants attention. No immediate dose modifications indicated. Metformin held on chemotherapy days but otherwise continued; patient instructed to hold metformin if symptomatic illness or procedures requiring contrast.

Plan: Monitor creatinine and urine protein/creatinine with each cycle. Consult nephrology if eGFR falls below 50 mL/min or proteinuria becomes significant (PCR >2.0).

6. GOALS OF CARE / OVERALL TRAJECTORY
I spent approximately 20 minutes speaking with Mr. Kowalski and his wife about the overall picture. They are relieved by the imaging results and encouraged by the tumor marker declines. I have been transparent that this is a serious illness with a Stage IV designation and that the current treatment is with palliative intent, though a hepatic resection remains a possibility we are working toward. The patient understands that complete cure is not guaranteed but remains optimistic and engaged. He is motivated to continue aggressive treatment. I emphasized that his quality of life and functional status are as important as the oncologic endpoints, and that neuropathy management is a priority for preserving his ability to work and perform daily activities.

Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 58-year-old male with KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases, presenting today for restaging review and treatment planning following completion of 12 cycles of mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Mr. Kowalski is a pleasant 58-year-old former smoker (20 pack-year history, quit 8 years ago) with Stage IV sigmoid colon cancer, initially diagnosed approximately 6 months ago when he presented with hematochezia, altered bowel habits, and a 12-pound unintentional weight loss. Colonoscopy at that time identified a partially obstructing 4.5 cm sigmoid mass; biopsy confirmed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. Staging CT and PET-CT revealed synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases in four segments (II, IV, VI, and VII; largest 3.8 cm in segment VI) with no pulmonary or peritoneal involvement at that time. Tumor molecular profiling identified KRAS G12D mutation (VAF 42.3%), TP53 R248W, APC truncating mutation, SMAD4 R361H, and PIK3CA E545K co-alteration; MSS by PCR; TMB 6.2 mut/Mb (low). RAS-mutant status precluded anti-EGFR therapy.

The patient was initiated on mFOLFOX6 (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, 5-FU 400 mg/m2 bolus + 2400 mg/m2 over 46 hours) plus bevacizumab 5 mg/kg every 14 days with a stated intent of converting hepatic metastases to resectability, or at minimum achieving durable disease control. The primary sigmoid tumor was left in situ, as the patient declined diverting colostomy and was managing adequately on a high-fiber diet given only partial luminal obstruction.

Mid-treatment restaging at cycle 6 demonstrated excellent partial response: hepatic lesions reduced 45-55% in linear dimension, primary sigmoid mass reduced approximately 31%, with CEA falling from 187.4 to 54.2 ng/mL and CA 19-9 falling from 412 to 138 U/mL. Since cycle 6, the patient continued on cycles 7-12 with several important toxicity events. After cycle 9, he developed Grade 3 oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy - severe paresthesias and numbness in both hands and feet, inability to button shirts or use a keyboard comfortably, and mild gait instability on uneven surfaces. No peritoneal implants, no adrenal lesions, and no osseous disease identified. The cumulative tumor shrinkage from baseline represents roughly a 58% reduction across measurable hepatic target lesions. CEA has fallen from 187.4 at baseline to 28.6 ng/mL; CA 19-9 from 412 to 72 U/mL.

The case was re-reviewed at multidisciplinary tumor board this week. The hepatic surgery team acknowledges that the hepatic disease remains technically resectable, with the caveat that the residual segment II lesion's proximity to the left hepatic vein continues to present margin concerns. Adequate future liver remnant is estimated. However, the primary sigmoid tumor has not been resected; simultaneous or staged colorectal and hepatic resection was discussed. Given the patient's ongoing neuropathy, the cumulative treatment course, and the patient's own stated preference to continue systemic therapy in the context of continued tumor response, the surgical team agrees that deferring resection at this time is reasonable.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS, Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with bilobar hepatic metastases - partial response maintained after 12 cycles of mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab.

The patient has completed 12 cycles of induction mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab and has achieved a meaningful, durable partial response - his hepatic disease has shrunk by more than half and his tumor markers have fallen to roughly 15% of their original values. This is a gratifying result. However, continuation of oxaliplatin beyond 12 cycles in the setting of established Grade 2-3 cumulative neuropathy (which only partially reversed with dose reduction) is not appropriate. We had anticipated this transition and the plan to de-escalate from oxaliplatin while maintaining disease control is consistent with the OPTIMOX1 strategy.

After discussion with the patient and review of the imaging and toxicity data, the multidisciplinary team and patient are aligned on the following:

- TRANSITION TO MAINTENANCE THERAPY: We will discontinue oxaliplatin and transition to maintenance simplified LV5FU2 (leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1, 5-FU 400 mg/m2 IV bolus followed by 2400 mg/m2 continuous infusion over 46 hours) plus bevacizumab 5 mg/kg IV every 14 days. This approach preserves the 5-FU backbone and anti-angiogenic activity while eliminating further cumulative oxaliplatin neurotoxicity. Expect that peripheral neuropathy will gradually improve off oxaliplatin over the coming weeks to months.

- HEPATIC RESECTION: Deferred at this time per patient preference and ongoing response. Will reassess at subsequent restaging. If tumor markers plateau or begin to rise on maintenance therapy without imaging evidence of response, we will re-engage surgical oncology for hepatic resection evaluation. Colorectal and hepatic resection sequence to be discussed with the surgical team if and when this becomes relevant.

- RESTAGING: Plan to restage with CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (CT chest as clinically indicated) every 8-10 weeks on maintenance therapy. Will obtain repeat CEA and CA 19-9 at each visit.

- FUTURE SECOND-LINE CONSIDERATIONS: Patient is aware that KRAS G12D represents a targetable alteration with emerging investigational therapies. No approved KRAS G12D inhibitor for CRC exists at this time. Patient's enrollment information in the KRAS G12D trial registry has been maintained. If and when disease progresses on maintenance, second-line therapy would favor an irinotecan-based regimen, likely FOLFIRI, with a vascular-targeted agent (ramucirumab or bevacizumab continuation per applicable trial data). TAS-102 and regorafenib held for later lines.

2. Grade 2 oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy, improving.
   - Duloxetine 60 mg daily continued.
   - Expect gradual improvement following oxaliplatin discontinuation; patient counseled that full recovery may take many months and may be incomplete.
   - Will monitor neuropathy at each visit; repeat EMG/NCS in 3-4 months to document trajectory.

3. Bevacizumab-associated hypertension, Grade 2-3.
   - Current BP 148/92 mmHg on lisinopril 20 mg daily and amlodipine 10 mg daily - improved from recent peaks but still above goal.
   - Continue dual antihypertensive therapy. Home blood pressure monitoring twice daily (mornings and evenings) and log to bring to next visit.
   - If systolic BP remains consistently above 150 mmHg at next visit, will consider adding a low-dose thiazide or uptitrating current agents in coordination with primary care.
   - Renal function monitored (eGFR stable at 71).

4. Type 2 diabetes mellitus.
   - Well-controlled on metformin 1000 mg BID; HbA1c 6.9%.
   - Continue current regimen. Renal function stable; metformin appropriate at current eGFR.

5. Hypertension (pre-existing, now complicated by bevacizumab therapy).
   - See above.

6. Mild normocytic anemia.
   - Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL, stable. Attributed to chemotherapy and chronic inflammation. No iron deficiency noted. No transfusion needed at this time. Will continue to monitor; may improve off oxaliplatin.

7. Hypomagnesemia (mild).
   - Prescribing magnesium oxide 400 mg PO twice daily. Recheck magnesium in 2 weeks.

8. Goals of care.
   - Patient has a clear understanding of his diagnosis (incurable Stage IV disease) and desires continued aggressive treatment. This is consistent with his overall functional status (ECOG PS 1) and his goals. His advance directive has been updated. Wife remains his healthcare proxy. No hospice or palliative care referral at this time; patient is actively engaged in oncologic management.

Return to clinic in 2 weeks for first maintenance LV5FU2 plus bevacizumab infusion and to review blood pressure log. Labs at that visit (CBC, CMP, CEA, CA 19-9). Patient verbalized understanding of the plan and has no further questions today. He expressed relief about stopping the oxaliplatin and cautious optimism about the maintenance phase.

Mr. Kowalski is a 58-year-old man with KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS, Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma who has developed clear radiographic and biochemical progression through maintenance LV5FU2 plus bevacizumab. The new bilateral pulmonary nodules are the most notable development, representing likely metastatic spread to a new organ system beyond the liver. CEA has tripled to 94.7 ng/mL and CA 19-9 has risen nearly four-fold over roughly 6-8 weeks, consistent with active systemic disease acceleration.

1. DISEASE PROGRESSION -- SECOND-LINE THERAPY PLAN
The patient has progressed through first-line induction mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab (12 cycles) and subsequent maintenance LV5FU2 plus bevacizumab. This is a clear failure of fluoropyrimidine-based therapy with ongoing VEGF-pathway inhibition.

Key considerations for second-line therapy selection:
- KRAS G12D mutation: Precludes use of anti-EGFR agents (cetuximab, panitumumab) outright. No KRAS G12D-targeted therapy is currently FDA-approved for colorectal cancer (sotorasib and adagrasib have very limited and underwhelming CRC response data compared to NSCLC; KRAS G12D-specific inhibitors such as MRTX1133 are in early clinical development).
- MSS/MMR-proficient: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab, nivolumab) are not indicated; responses in MSS CRC are exceedingly rare.
- Prior bevacizumab: Per the RAISE trial, continuation of VEGF pathway inhibition via a different mechanism (VEGFR2 blockade with ramucirumab) in combination with irinotecan-based FOLFIRI demonstrated an overall survival benefit compared to FOLFIRI alone in the second-line setting. This remains a standard-of-care option.

Plan: Initiate FOLFIRI (irinotecan/leucovorin/5-FU) plus ramucirumab 8 mg/kg IV on day 1 of each 2-week cycle.

UGT1A1 genotyping will be ordered prior to first irinotecan dose. If UGT1A1*28 homozygous, will start irinotecan at a reduced dose (120-150 mg/m2); if heterozygous or wild-type, will start at 150-180 mg/m2 with uptitration as tolerated. Standard antiemetic and antidiarrheal prophylaxis will be provided.

Risks discussed with patient and his wife: diarrhea (including severe Grade 3-4, especially with UGT1A1 variants), myelosuppression (particularly neutropenia), alopecia, nausea/vomiting, fatigue, mucositis. Ramucirumab-specific risks: hypertension (concerning given his current bevacizumab-related hypertension already requiring dual antihypertensives), proteinuria, wound healing impairment, arterial thromboembolic events, infusion reactions.

2. PULMONARY NODULES -- TISSUE CONFIRMATION
Given Mr. Kowalski's prior 20 pack-year smoking history (quit 8 years ago), a primary lung malignancy cannot be assumed to be excluded based on imaging morphology alone, even in the setting of known CRC metastases. The new nodules are morphologically non-specific (solid, sub-centimeter range). To avoid misattributing a primary lung tumor to metastatic CRC (with implications for staging, histology, and potentially actionable lung-specific mutations such as EGFR, ALK, ROS1), I am referring for CT-guided biopsy of the right upper lobe nodule (largest and most accessible). Interventional radiology consultation placed.

Liquid biopsy (ctDNA) also ordered to confirm persistence of KRAS G12D variant allele in circulating tumor DNA, which would further support CRC-origin for the pulmonary lesions.

3. HYPERTENSION -- ONGOING MANAGEMENT
BP today 158/102 mmHg despite lisinopril 20 mg and amlodipine 10 mg daily. With planned ramucirumab (VEGFR2 inhibitor), hypertension will likely worsen further -- this is a recognized class effect. Discussing with nephrology/cardiology regarding addition of a third antihypertensive agent (likely hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily or chlorthalidone) prior to ramucirumab initiation. Will check urine protein/creatinine ratio today (above). If BP not controlled to <140/90 before cycle 1, will consider brief ramucirumab hold per protocol guidelines. Ongoing co-management with internal medicine/nephrology.

4. MILD ANEMIA
Hgb 11.4 g/dL, mild. Iron studies and reticulocyte count ordered. If iron deficiency contributing, will consider IV iron supplementation (oral iron poorly tolerated with GI malignancy and prior GI symptoms). Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents not indicated in the context of active chemotherapy for CRC.

5. HEPATIC FUNCTION MONITORING
Mild transaminase elevation (AST 44, ALT 51) and elevated alkaline phosphatase (128 U/L) are likely related to hepatic metastases rather than medication hepatotoxicity. Will monitor closely with each cycle. Metformin continues to be appropriate at current eGFR of 72 mL/min/1.73m2; will reassess if renal function declines further (threshold for holding metformin <45 mL/min).

6. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY
Grade 1 residual oxaliplatin neuropathy, significantly improved from prior Grade 2-3. Duloxetine 60 mg daily will be continued for neuropathic symptom management and possible mood benefit. FOLFIRI does not have a neuropathy component; irinotecan's primary neurologic toxicity is cholinergic (early diarrhea, diaphoresis, lacrimation during or shortly after infusion, managed with atropine premedication).

7. KRAS G12D TARGETED THERAPY -- CLINICAL TRIAL MONITORING
Discussed the current landscape of KRAS G12D-targeted therapy in CRC. No approved KRAS G12D-specific agents exist for CRC at this time. The response rates seen with covalent KRAS G12C inhibitors (sotorasib, adagrasib) in CRC are modest (10-20%) due to upfront and acquired resistance mechanisms mediated through EGFR bypass signaling, which is particularly prominent in the CRC microenvironment. KRAS G12D inhibitors are mechanistically more challenging given the need for non-covalent binding strategies. Early-phase trials combining KRAS inhibitors with MEK inhibitors (e.g., KRAS G12D inhibitor plus binimetinib combinations) are emerging as a strategy to overcome MAPK pathway reactivation. I will maintain the patient's information with our clinical trials office. Following tissue confirmation of pulmonary metastases and after establishing stability or disease assessment on FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab (typically 2-4 cycles), clinical trial enrollment may be considered for patients failing second-line therapy.

8. GOALS OF CARE AND PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT
Had a frank and compassionate discussion with Mr. Kowalski and his wife today regarding the implications of progression. Reinforced that this remains an incurable diagnosis; however, active systemic therapy options exist and we will continue to pursue them aggressively consistent with his wishes. He remains fully engaged and motivated. He has completed an advance directive and his wife is aware of his healthcare proxy designation. He will continue to work part-time as a high school history teacher, which he finds meaningful. Referral to palliative care for symptom support (fatigue, dyspnea, psychosocial support) offered -- patient will consider. Social work referral placed.

9. FOLLOW-UP
Return visit in 2 weeks for cycle 1 FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab, pending UGT1A1 genotyping results and blood pressure optimization. Tumor markers (CEA, CA 19-9) to be checked at each cycle. Restaging CT planned after 4 cycles (approximately 8-10 weeks). Lung biopsy scheduled through interventional radiology within the next 7-10 days.

Time spent with patient and family: 55 minutes (>50% of time in counseling and coordination of care).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Histologic sections of the core biopsy material reveal fragments of pulmonary parenchyma infiltrated by a malignant epithelial neoplasm. The tumor is arranged in irregular glandular structures with complex, cribriform, and back-to-back architecture. The neoplastic glands are lined by columnar to cuboidal cells with pseudostratified, hyperchromatic, elongated nuclei, prominent nucleoli, and moderate-to-abundant pale eosinophilic cytoplasm. Nuclear pleomorphism is moderate. Mitotic figures are readily identified, including atypical forms. Intraluminal necrotic debris ("dirty necrosis") is present within multiple glandular spaces, a feature highly characteristic of colorectal adenocarcinoma. There is no evidence of acinar formation with lepidic spread, papillary architecture, or mucin-secreting goblet cell morphology that would suggest a primary pulmonary adenocarcinoma.

The background lung parenchyma shows reactive type II pneumocyte hyperplasia and mild interstitial fibrosis. There is no evidence of an in-situ lepidic component. No granulomas are identified. No fungal organisms or acid-fast bacilli are seen on special stains.

The overall morphology is most consistent with metastatic adenocarcinoma from a colorectal primary.

  - Morphology: columnar glandular architecture with intraluminal dirty necrosis
    and nuclear pseudostratification consistent with colorectal origin.
  - Immunophenotype: CDX2+, CK20+, CK7 focally+, TTF-1-, Napsin A-, SATB2+;
    primary lung adenocarcinoma excluded.
  - No evidence of an in-situ or lepidic growth component to suggest primary
    pulmonary origin.
  - No granulomas or infectious organisms identified.
  - Correlation with clinical history of Stage IV KRAS G12D-mutant sigmoid
    adenocarcinoma with known hepatic metastases is recommended and supportive.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
COMMENT
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The histomorphologic and immunohistochemical findings are diagnostic of metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma in this pulmonary nodule. The IHC profile, particularly diffuse CDX2 and CK20 positivity combined with negative TTF-1 and Napsin A, is highly specific for colorectal origin and effectively excludes a primary lung adenocarcinoma. SATB2 positivity provides additional confirmatory support for lower gastrointestinal primary origin. The focal CK7 staining is within the expected range of minor co-expression seen in a subset of colorectal carcinomas and is not sufficient to suggest a CK7-predominant primary.

In the context of this patient's 20-pack-year smoking history, the exclusion of a synchronous primary lung malignancy is clinically significant and supports re-staging as metastatic colorectal cancer with pulmonary involvement rather than a second primary.

No primary hepatocellular carcinoma morphology (trabecular growth pattern, bile production) is identified. No cholangiocarcinomatous differentiation is seen. No lymphovascular invasion is identified within the core sample, though this is limited by tissue volume. The non-neoplastic hepatic parenchyma shows no significant steatosis, fibrosis, or cirrhosis.

The combined histologic and immunohistochemical findings from Specimens A and B establish a diagnosis of moderately differentiated colorectal adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon with synchronous hepatic metastasis. The morphology is classical for colorectal adenocarcinoma, including back-to-back glands, nuclear pseudostratification, frequent mitoses, and intraluminal dirty necrosis. The CDX2-positive, CK20-positive, CK7-negative immunophenotype strongly supports a lower gastrointestinal tract primary and effectively excludes hepatocellular carcinoma, primary lung adenocarcinoma, pancreatobiliary adenocarcinoma (typically CK7+/CK20-/CDX2-), and upper GI adenocarcinoma.

Retained expression of all four mismatch repair proteins (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) by immunohistochemistry indicates a proficient MMR (pMMR) phenotype. This result predicts a microsatellite stable (MSS) tumor, which is associated with a lower likelihood of response to immune checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy and confirms the tumor is not Lynch syndrome-associated by protein expression criteria. However, formal germline testing should be considered in appropriate clinical contexts.

KRAS, NRAS, and BRAF mutational analysis results are pending and will be issued as an addendum. These results are critical for guiding anti-EGFR therapy eligibility (cetuximab, panitumumab). Comprehensive NGS panel results will also be reported separately by the reference laboratory.

IMPRESSION:

1. Bilateral pulmonary nodules (right upper lobe 7 mm, right lower lobe 5 mm, left upper lobe 6 mm), each slightly smaller compared to the prior progression study, consistent with an early favorable response to systemic therapy in the appropriate clinical context of known metastatic colorectal carcinoma. No new pulmonary nodules.

2. Bilobar hepatic metastatic disease demonstrates a modest but consistent reduction in the size of the three measurable lesions (segment VI, II, and IV), with the segment VII lesion remaining below the threshold of reliable measurement. Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 59-year-old male with known metastatic KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma presenting for scheduled follow-up and restaging review after 4 cycles of second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab. He is here today to review CT imaging results from earlier this week.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Kowalski is a 59-year-old Polish-American former smoker (20 pack-year history, quit approximately 8 years ago) who was diagnosed with Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma approximately 13 months ago. His disease is characterized by a KRAS p.G12D mutation, BRAF and NRAS wild-type status, microsatellite stable (MSS) phenotype, and low tumor mutational burden (6.2 mut/Mb), which precluded the use of anti-EGFR therapy or checkpoint immunotherapy at any point in his treatment course. SMAD4 mutation and PIK3CA E545K were also identified on his initial comprehensive NGS panel.

To briefly summarize his treatment history: he was initiated on first-line mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab approximately 13 months ago, achieving an excellent partial response after 6 and 12 cycles with near-complete response of the segment VII hepatic lesion. He completed 12 cycles of mFOLFOX6 but was transitioned to maintenance LV5FU2 plus bevacizumab at that point due to Grade 3 oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy. While on maintenance therapy, his disease remained stable for approximately 2 months, but at the 10-month mark, CT imaging demonstrated progressive disease with new bilateral pulmonary nodules (confirmed to represent metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma on CT-guided biopsy of the right upper lobe nodule - TTF-1 negative, CDX2 positive, CK20 positive), enlargement of the known hepatic metastases in segments II, IV, and VI, growth of the primary sigmoid mass, and new perihepatic lymphadenopathy. His CEA had risen sharply to 94.7 ng/mL at that time.

Given RAS-mutant disease precluding anti-EGFR options, he was transitioned to second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab approximately 2.5 months ago, consistent with the RAISE trial data supporting the addition of ramucirumab to FOLFIRI in the second-line setting. UGT1A1 genotyping prior to initiation revealed *1/*28 heterozygosity (intermediate metabolizer), and he was started at a reduced irinotecan dose of 150 mg/m2 with intention to uptitrate as tolerated. After cycle 2, he developed Grade 2 diarrhea and Grade 2 fatigue, necessitating a further irinotecan dose reduction to 135 mg/m2; this has been maintained through cycles 3 and 4 without escalation. He also developed worsening hypertension (Grade 3) in the setting of ramucirumab (anti-VEGFR2), requiring the addition of hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily to his existing regimen of lisinopril 20 mg daily and amlodipine 10 mg daily.

Today's visit is for review of restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed 3 days ago after completion of 4 cycles of FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab.

Since his last visit, Mr. Kowalski reports that his overall functional status is reasonably maintained. He has returned to some light teaching duties but continues to work with reduced hours. His diarrhea has been managed adequately with dietary modification and intermittent loperamide use; he is averaging 2-3 loose stools per day above baseline (improved from prior Grade 2 episode). He denies frank watery diarrhea or incontinence. He reports mild fatigue, particularly in the 5-6 days following each infusion, but notes that he has been recovering in time for the next cycle. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast, reviewed today:

The CT imaging from this restaging shows encouraging early response to FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab. All three bilateral pulmonary nodules have slightly decreased in size compared with the progression scan: the right upper lobe nodule is down to 7 mm (from 8 mm), the right lower lobe nodule is 5 mm (from 6 mm), and the left upper lobe nodule is 6 mm (from 7 mm). While these are modest reductions that may partly reflect measurement variability, taken together with the hepatic findings and falling tumor markers, they appear to reflect a genuine treatment response rather than measurement artifact.

The hepatic lesions have more clearly decreased: the dominant segment VI lesion is now 2.1 x 1.8 cm, down from 2.4 x 2.1 cm at progression. The segment II lesion has come down to 1.0 x 0.8 cm from 1.2 x 1.0 cm, and the segment IV lesion is 1.2 x 0.9 cm, down from 1.4 x 1.1 cm. ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Kowalski is a 59-year-old male with Stage IV KRAS G12D-mutant, BRAF wild-type, MSS sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with bilobar hepatic metastases and pulmonary metastases (confirmed metastatic CRC on lung biopsy). He is 13 months out from initial diagnosis and is currently receiving second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab at modified irinotecan dosing (135 mg/m2, UGT1A1 *1/*28 heterozygous).

1. METASTATIC COLORECTAL CANCER - EARLY PARTIAL RESPONSE TO SECOND-LINE FOLFIRI PLUS RAMUCIRUMAB
The restaging CT today shows encouraging early response - hepatic lesion shrinkage, modest pulmonary nodule decrease, and no new sites of disease. Tumor markers (CEA and CA 19-9) are both trending down meaningfully from the progression peak. This is consistent with an objective partial response to FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab, which is gratifying given the modest response rates typically seen in RAS-mutant MSS disease.

Plan: Continue FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab at current doses. Will proceed with cycle 5 today pending counts (ANC adequate at 1.8, counts acceptable). No dose modifications are indicated at this time. Will continue monitoring irinotecan-related toxicities closely. Next restaging CT planned in 8-10 weeks (approximately after cycles 7-8). Will continue monitoring CEA and CA 19-9 at each cycle visit.

I discussed with Mr. Kowalski and his wife that while this is encouraging, second-line therapy for KRAS-mutant metastatic colorectal cancer remains palliative in intent and response durability is typically measured in months. The goal is to maintain disease control as long as possible while preserving quality of life. He understands this and remains motivated to continue treatment.

2. TREATMENT-RELATED HYPERTENSION (GRADE 2-3, ANTI-VEGFR2/ANTI-VEGF RELATED)
Blood pressure today is 146/94 mmHg, which represents an improvement from the Grade 3 values seen at cycle 2 visit (168/108 mmHg). With the addition of hydrochlorothiazide, he is now borderline Grade 2. Nephrology has been co-managing and has been satisfied with his current antihypertensive regimen (lisinopril 20 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, HCTZ 25 mg daily). Will continue monitoring at each visit. BP target less than 140/90 mmHg ideally; may need further optimization. Not a reason to hold ramucirumab at this time given improvement trend. Urine protein/creatinine ratio is 0.18, below the threshold of concern for ramucirumab-associated proteinuria.

3. IRINOTECAN-INDUCED DIARRHEA / UGT1A1 PHARMACOGENOMICS
Grade 1-2 diarrhea is manageable at current irinotecan dose of 135 mg/m2. Averages 2-3 loose stools per day above baseline on infusion weeks. Instructed on continued dietary modifications (low-residue diet during infusion weeks), maintained loperamide rescue protocol. Will not uptitrate irinotecan dose given prior Grade 2 toxicity and UGT1A1 heterozygosity; may dose reduce further if diarrhea escalates. Patient cautioned to call with any increase in stool frequency above 6 per day or if associated with fever, dehydration, or inability to tolerate oral intake.

4. RESIDUAL OXALIPLATIN-INDUCED PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY
Neuropathy has improved from Grade 2-3 at its nadir to Grade 1 currently. Mild fingertip numbness bilaterally, not functionally limiting. Duloxetine 60 mg daily ongoing - patient reports this has helped with symptom management. Will continue monitoring. No new neurologic concerns on today's exam. Not affecting his ability to receive FOLFIRI (irinotecan does not cause cumulative neuropathy).

5. TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS
Metformin 1000 mg twice daily continued. No hypoglycemia reported. Glycemic management stable; will check HbA1c at next visit. Renal function stable (eGFR 74), metformin appropriate.

6. HYPERLIPIDEMIA
Atorvastatin continued. No dose changes needed. LFTs mildly elevated but attributable to hepatic metastases rather than statin toxicity; consistent with prior pattern.

7. KRAS G12D TARGETED THERAPY / CLINICAL TRIAL PLANNING
Patient remains interested in KRAS G12D-targeted investigational therapy. We discussed the current landscape: KRAS G12D-specific inhibitors are distinct from the KRAS G12C inhibitors (sotorasib, adagrasib) that are approved in lung cancer and show modest activity in colorectal cancer. KRAS G12D is the most common KRAS mutation in colorectal cancer and represents an active area of investigational effort. We are tracking his eligibility for available clinical trials and he is registered with an NCI-designated cancer center trial. However, given the current partial response to FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab, it is appropriate to continue the current effective regimen and preserve the clinical trial option for a future line of therapy. Patient agrees with this approach. We will revisit clinical trial options at the time of progression or intolerable toxicity.

8. GOALS OF CARE / SUPPORTIVE CARE
Patient and wife are engaged and well-informed. Patient understands the incurable nature of his disease and continues to desire aggressive, active treatment. Advance directive is completed and on file. He is coping reasonably well emotionally; his wife is his primary support person. Social work referral offered and declined at this time. Palliative care co-management discussed - patient willing to meet with the palliative care team in a supportive capacity for symptom management, not exclusively end-of-life planning; referral placed.

FOLLOW-UP: Return in 2 weeks for cycle 5 pre-treatment labs and FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab administration. Routine labs (CBC with differential, CMP, CEA, CA 19-9, urine protein/creatinine) drawn at each infusion visit. Next restaging CT in 8-10 weeks. CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 59-year-old male with metastatic KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma presenting for post-operative oncology follow-up approximately 4 weeks after laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy. The primary purpose of today's visit is to assess surgical recovery, review final pathology from the resection specimen, discuss the trajectory of his metastatic disease, and plan transition to clinical trial enrollment for a KRAS G12D-targeted investigational protocol.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Kowalski is well known to this service. By way of summary, he is a 58-year-old male (now 59) who initially presented with a partially obstructing sigmoid colon mass and synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases, staged as cT4aN2bM1a (Stage IV) adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon. Biomarker profiling established KRAS exon 2, codon 12, p.G12D mutation; NRAS and BRAF wild-type; MMR-proficient/MSS; HER2 non-amplified; TMB low at 6.2 mut/Mb. CEA at diagnosis was 187.4 ng/mL, CA 19-9 was 412 U/mL. The KRAS G12D mutation definitively precluded anti-EGFR therapy.

He was treated with first-line mFOLFOX6 plus bev for 12 cycles, achieving a meaningful partial response in both hepatic and primary lesions (CEA nadir to approximately 28 ng/mL). Cumulative oxali-induced peripheral neuropathy (Grade 3 at worst) necessitated dose reduction and ultimately discontinuation of oxali after cycle 9/10. He transitioned to maintenance LV5FU2 plus Avastin per OPTIMOX strategy. During maintenance, he developed radiographic progression at approximately 10 months, with new bilateral pulmonary metastases and regrowth of hepatic lesions, accompanied by a sharp rise in CEA to 94.7 ng/mL. CT-guided biopsy of a right upper lobe pulmonary nodule confirmed metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma (CDX2+, CK20+, TTF-1 negative), ruling out a primary lung malignancy given his former smoking history.

He was transitioned to second-line FOLFIRI plus ramu. UGT1A1 genotyping showed *1/*28 heterozygosity (intermediate metabolizer), prompting a dose-attenuated start. He achieved an early partial response after 4 cycles (CEA to 61.3 ng/mL), subsequently maintaining stable disease through 8 cycles. However, iri dose reductions were required following a Grade 3 diarrhea/neutropenia hospitalization after cycle 6. Persistent treatment-related hypertension from Avastin and subsequently ramu has required escalating antihypertensive therapy (now on triple agents: lisinopril 20 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily).

Approximately 16 months into his disease course, the multidisciplinary team and patient agreed to proceed with laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy for local control, given intermittent partial colonic obstruction, hematochezia, and the need for pre-trial surgical staging for the KRAS G12D inhibitor protocol. bev and ramu were held 4 weeks pre-operatively per standard anti-angiogenic surgery hold protocol. He underwent uncomplicated laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with primary anastomosis approximately 4 weeks ago (estimated blood loss 120 mL, no intraoperative complications).

Today he returns for post-operative oncology evaluation. He reports overall good recovery. He is tolerating a regular diet without difficulty, bowel function has returned to baseline (1-2 formed stools per day), and he has resumed most activities of daily living. He notes persistent but manageable residual fatigue, which he attributes to the operation and the cumulative effects of approximately 16 months of systemic chemotherapy. He reports no fever, chills, wound complications, or symptoms of anastomotic leak. The residual peripheral neuropathy from prior oxali therapy is stable - described as mild tingling and numbness at the fingertips bilaterally, not interfering with daily activities at this point. He has resumed his antihypertensive medications and blood pressure has been running in the 138-148 systolic range at home on his log.

He is eager to initiate the KRAS G12D inhibitor clinical trial. He was pre-screened and found eligible prior to surgery: KRAS G12D-mutant CRC confirmed on both tumor tissue and ctDNA (VAF 3.8% at progression), ECOG 0-1, prior exposure to fluoropyrimidine and Eloxatin, no prior KRAS-targeted therapy.

1. METASTATIC KRAS G12D-MUTANT, MSS SIGMOID COLON ADENOCARCINOMA (Stage IVB, ypT3N1bM1b), s/p laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy:

Mr. Kowalski has now completed surgical resection of his primary tumor and is approximately 4 weeks post-op with excellent recovery. His metastatic disease burden includes stable bilobar hepatic lesions (three measurable lesions in segments II, IV, VI) and three bilateral pulmonary nodules that have remained stable on FOLFIRI plus ramu. His overall disease trajectory is that of a patient who has achieved meaningful but not complete disease control on two lines of therapy, now 18 months from diagnosis. For KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS metastatic CRC, this is consistent with the expected pattern - median overall survival in the 18-24 month range historically, though outcomes continue to improve with access to novel targeted approaches.

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Sections from the tumor bed demonstrate a 2.7 cm treatment bed with extensive hyalinized fibrosis, acellular mucin pools (some with associated foreign body giant cell reaction), hemosiderin-laden macrophages, and a background of chronic lymphocytic and plasmacytic inflammatory infiltrate. These features are consistent with treatment response to prior systemic chemotherapy (fluoropyrimidine/oxaliplatin/irinotecan-based regimens and anti-angiogenic agents).

Residual viable adenocarcinoma is identified as irregular, moderately differentiated glandular structures (tubular and cribriform architecture) with nuclear pseudostratification, luminal dirty necrosis, nuclear enlargement, and prominent nucleoli. Viable tumor cells are scattered irregularly throughout the fibrotic treatment bed and focally at the invasive front extending into the pericolorectal adipose tissue. Tumor budding is identified focally (Bd2 per ITBCC 2016 criteria: 5-9 buds per 0.785 mm2 hot spot). Overall, residual viable tumor comprises approximately 30-35% of the total treatment bed area, consistent with Ryan/AJCC Tumor Regression Grade 2 (moderate response: >10% but <50% viable tumor remains).

The tumor penetrates through the full thickness of the muscularis propria and extends into the pericolorectal adipose tissue. The visceral peritoneum (serosa) is not breached; the closest serosal approach by tumor is approximately 3.5 mm. Depth of invasion beyond the muscularis propria is up to 8 mm into pericolorectal fat.

Lymphovascular invasion is confirmed: D2-40 immunostain highlights dilated lymphatic channels containing intraluminal tumor cell clusters in the pericolorectal adipose tissue adjacent to the treatment bed. Small vessel (vascular) invasion by tumor is not definitively identified on H&E or D2-40/CD31.

Perineural invasion is identified at the deep invasive front; a large-caliber nerve bundle measuring approximately 0.4 mm in diameter shows near-circumferential tumor infiltration.

Two of thirteen (2/13) pericolorectal lymph nodes are positive for metastatic adenocarcinoma (see above). One additional lymph node (included in the 11 negative) shows acellular mucin pools with fibrosis but no viable tumor cells, interpreted as treated metastatic disease with complete histologic response in that node; this node is classified as ypN0 per AJCC 8th edition convention (acellular mucin pools following treatment are not counted as viable metastasis).

Proximal and distal resection margins are negative for carcinoma. The circumferential radial margin is negative (tumor is 2.1 mm from the inked margin at the closest point).

The non-neoplastic sigmoid colon mucosa shows mild chronic colitis/architectural distortion, likely related to prior partial obstruction and ischemic changes. No dysplasia is identified in the background mucosa. No adenomatous polyps are seen in this specimen.

This sigmoid colon resection demonstrates a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma

INTEGRATED GENOMIC INTERPRETATION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

This tumor demonstrates a genomic profile consistent with the chromosomal
instability (CIN) subtype of sporadic colorectal adenocarcinoma, with a
molecular architecture typical of left-sided, RAS-mutant metastatic CRC.
The co-occurring mutations in KRAS (G12D), TP53 (R248W), and APC (Q1367*)
represent the canonical "Vogelstein pathway" of colorectal carcinogenesis
(APC loss → RAS activation → TP53 inactivation). The additional somatic
alterations in SMAD4, PIK3CA, FBXW7, and ARID1A represent secondary
clonal events likely contributing to metastatic progression. Homozygous
CDKN2A deletion and MYC amplification reinforce aggressive proliferative
and anti-apoptotic tumor biology.

The high VAFs of KRAS (42.3%), TP53 (39.1%), and APC (44.7%) — all near
or above 40% in a tumor with estimated 55% cellularity — indicate these
are clonal, founder-level events present in essentially all tumor cells.
Lower VAF alterations including SMAD4 (18.2%), PIK3CA (11.4%), FBXW7
(9.8%), TCF7L2 (8.1%), and ARID1A (7.3%) likely represent subclonal
events, potentially reflecting intratumoral heterogeneity or selective
pressure during metastatic dissemination.

The SMAD4 p.R361H mutation (VAF 18.2%) is particularly noteworthy in
the context of synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases, consistent with
the established role of SMAD4 loss in promoting hematogenous spread in
colorectal carcinoma.
